provenance.source_corpus: Ecosystem response to changes in water level of Lake Ontario marshes: lessons from the restoration of Cootes Paradise Marsh

nodes.name: Removal of most of the common-carp population from Cootes Paradise

source_spans.text: Removal of most of the common-carp population from Cootes Paradise resulted in a predictable decrease in TSS of about 25%, after the effects of inter-annual differences in water level was taken into account (Fig. 4).

Removal of most of the common-carp population from Cootes Paradise resulted in a predictable decrease in TSS of about 25%, after the effects of inter-annual differences in water level was taken into account (Fig. 4).
A non-linear regression equation explained close to 90% of the variation compared with 80% for a non-linear equation, and this trend was confirmed for the dominant species, Typha latifolia.
A non-linear regression equation explained close to 90% of the variation compared with 80% for a non-linear equation, and this trend was confirmed for the dominant species, Typha latifolia.
Loss of emergent vegetation has been attributed to sustained high water levels over the past 30 years
Loss of emergent vegetation has been attributed to sustained high water levels over the past 30 years
During years of low water-level, fish cannot access the coastal marshes to spawn
During years of low water-level, fish cannot access the coastal marshes to spawn
common carp ( Cyprinus carpio), a benthivorous feeder that grows well in the eutrophic, turbid conditions of the marsh, and whose feeding and spawning behaviour keep the waters turbid
common carp ( Cyprinus carpio), a benthivorous feeder that grows well in the eutrophic, turbid conditions of the marsh, and whose feeding and spawning behaviour keep the waters turbid
Besides resuspending sediment and keeping water turbidity high, carp can also uproot submergent plants
Besides resuspending sediment and keeping water turbidity high, carp can also uproot submergent plants
The disappearance of submergent vegetation is thought to be due to poor light penetration that is assumed to be limiting germination and growth of seedlings and propagules.
The disappearance of submergent vegetation is thought to be due to poor light penetration that is assumed to be limiting germination and growth of seedlings and propagules.
Large Daphnia, which had been released from predation in early summer, reached unprecedented numbers in the open-water areas, and grazed down the algal community
Large Daphnia, which had been released from predation in early summer, reached unprecedented numbers in the open-water areas, and grazed down the algal community
Water-level fluctuations are known to contribute to the great diversity of aquatic vegetation in coastal wetlands
Water-level fluctuations are known to contribute to the great diversity of aquatic vegetation in coastal wetlands
A modest recovery of emergent vegetation in 1999 following carp exclusion could have been predicted from declining water level alone
A modest recovery of emergent vegetation in 1999 following carp exclusion could have been predicted from declining water level alone
